name: NSF-Prompt-Broader-Impacts
description: NSF Broader Impacts section - societal benefit, education, diversity, outreach, dissemination

variants.name: default
variants.text:
You are writing the BROADER IMPACTS section of an NSF grant proposal. INPUTS: - {{content}}: The researcher's actual work, publications, and technical expertise. This is your PRIMARY source for understanding what the research is about and what applications it enables. - {{grant_info}}: The specific NSF grant program description, priorities, and requirements. Use this to identify which application areas and societal needs the program cares about. YOUR TASK: Generate ONLY the Broader Impacts section. Do NOT generate a Project Summary, Intellectual Merit, Implementation Plan, References, Budget Justification, Data Management Plan, Facilities description, or Mentoring Plan. Those are handled separately. SECTION STRUCTURE — cover each of these subsections: 1. BENEFIT TO SOCIETY - Connect the specific research described in {{content}} to concrete societal challenges - Identify 2-3 primary application areas where this research will have impact - Reference specific application areas mentioned in {{grant_info}} where they align with the researcher's work - Include quantitative context where possible (e.g., market size, energy consumption statistics, prevalence of a problem) but do NOT fabricate statistics — use well-known figures or qualify with "approximately" - Be specific to the actual research — avoid generic statements that could apply to any proposal 2. EDUCATION AND TRAINING Graduate Education: - Training plan for graduate students supported by the project (1-2 students, consistent with single-PI CBET budget) - Specific technical skills they will develop based on the methods in {{content}} - Professional development activities (conferences, writing, presentations) Undergraduate Research: - Plan for engaging 1-2 undergraduate researchers per year - Specific roles they would play in the research from {{content}} - Connection to existing university programs (REU, honors thesis) — reference generically unless {{content}} names specific programs Curriculum Development: - 1-2 specific course modules or laboratory exercises derived from this research - How research results will be integrated into existing courses - Plans for making educational materials broadly available (e.g., nanoHUB, open courseware) 3. BROADENING PARTICIPATION OF UNDERREPRESENTED GROUPS - Specific recruitment strategies for graduate and undergraduate researchers from underrepresented groups - Mentoring structures that support retention and success - Outreach partnerships — reference generically (e.g., "partnership with regional HBCUs/HSIs") unless {{content}} names specific institutions - Scale these activities realistically for a single-PI research group — do NOT promise activities that would require a full-time outreach coordinator - Target specific, achievable numbers (e.g., "2 summer REU students per year from underrepresented groups" not "300-400 K-12 students annually") 4. K-12 OUTREACH AND PUBLIC ENGAGEMENT - 1-2 specific outreach activities tied to the research topic - Describe the activity concretely: who participates, what they do, how often, what they learn - Connection to the actual science in {{content}} — avoid generic STEM outreach descriptions - Realistic scope: 2-4 activities per year appropriate for a research lab, not an outreach center 5. DISSEMINATION - Publication plan: target number of papers and likely venues relevant to the field in {{content}} - Conference presentations: name 2-4 specific conferences relevant to the research field - Open science commitments: open-access publishing, preprints, open-source software, shared datasets - If the research produces software, tools, or databases, describe how they will be maintained and shared 6. INFRASTRUCTURE FOR RESEARCH AND EDUCATION - How equipment or capabilities developed will serve the broader community - Shared facility access or collaborative measurement opportunities - Databases, software, or educational resources that will persist beyond the award period WRITING GUIDELINES: - Write for expert peer reviewers in the specific field described in {{content}} - Every broader impact should connect back to the specific research — avoid generic broader impacts that could be copy-pasted into any proposal - Be realistic about scale: this is a single-PI CBET award with ~$120-150K/year, not a center grant - Do NOT assume the research involves artificial intelligence unless {{content}} explicitly describes AI/ML work - Do NOT fabricate names of partner institutions, collaborators, or specific programs not found in {{content}} - Do NOT promise outcomes you cannot quantify (e.g., "economic impact exceeding $10 billion") - If {{content}} mentions specific outreach activities, education efforts, or diversity initiatives the PI has done, highlight those as evidence of commitment and track record - If {{content}} does not provide information about the PI's outreach history, frame activities as "proposed" rather than "continuing" ALIGNMENT: - Read {{grant_info}} carefully for application areas the program specifically encourages (e.g., energy, environment, health, manufacturing) - Connect broader impacts to those specific program interests - If {{grant_info}} mentions CAREER proposals, note that the education plan could be expanded for a CAREER submission but keep the current scope appropriate for a standard award OUTPUT FORMAT: Generate well-structured HTML: - Use

for the section title "Broader Impacts" - Use

for each major subsection - Use

for sub-subsections where needed - Use

for paragraph content - Use

    /
      and
    1. for lists - Use for emphasis sparingly - Target approximately 3-5 pages of content (roughly 2,000-3,000 words)
